plainMeaning: Chanting the name of Lord Ajita, the supreme being, brings true well-being, and praising Lord Śānti, the best of Jinas, awakens steadiness and wisdom and brings peace.
recitation: ajia jiṇa suha-pavattaṇaṁ, tava purisuttama nāma kittaṇaṁ; taha ya dhii-mai-ppavattaṇaṁ, tava ya jiṇuttama saṁti kittaṇaṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ajia < Sanskrit a-jita, 'not conquered, invincible'; here the vocative naming the 2nd Tīrthaṅkara.
wordByWord.notes: Vocative; the name carries its literal sense 'invincible' alongside the proper name.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jiṇa < Sanskrit jina, 'one who has conquered' (root ji, 'to win').
Prakrit suha < Sanskrit sukha, 'ease, happiness'; first member of the compound suha-pavattaṇaṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: bringing-forth, setting in motion, that which promotes
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pavattaṇa < Sanskrit pravartana (pra + √vṛt), 'causing to proceed, promoting'; accusative/predicative -aṁ.
wordByWord.notes: suha-pavattaṇaṁ = 'productive of happiness' — qualifies the recitation of the name.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit/Sanskrit tava, genitive of tvam, 'of you'.
Prakrit purisuttama < Sanskrit puruṣottama (puruṣa + uttama), 'highest of beings'; vocative.
wordByWord.notes: An epithet of the Tīrthaṅkara, here addressed to Ajita.
Object of kittaṇaṁ: nāma-kittaṇaṁ, 'proclaiming of the name'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit kittaṇa < Sanskrit kīrtana (√kṝt, 'to mention, celebrate'); accusative -aṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Same root as kittaïssaṁ in Logassa; the act of singing the Jina's name.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit taha < Sanskrit tathā, 'thus, in that manner'.
wordByWord.notes: Opens the parallel second half, mirroring the first.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit dhii < Sanskrit dhṛti (√dhṛ, 'to hold firm'), 'firmness, constancy'.
Prakrit mai < Sanskrit mati (√man, 'to think'), 'mind, wisdom'.
wordByWord.notes: Read as mati ('intellect'), not the homophone 'to me'; paired with dhṛti as a virtue-couplet.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pavattaṇa < Sanskrit pravartana; initial conjunct doubled (ppa-) by sandhi after the preceding vowel.
wordByWord.notes: Doubled conjunct preserved verbatim per the book's orthography; dhii-mai-ppavattaṇaṁ = 'productive of fortitude and wisdom'.
Second occurrence, now addressed to Śānti.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jiṇuttama < Sanskrit jinottama (jina + uttama), 'highest of the conquerors'; vocative.
wordByWord.notes: Parallels purisuttama; here directed at Śānti.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit saṁti < Sanskrit śānti (√śam, 'to be calm'), 'peace, tranquillity'.
wordByWord.notes: Bivalent: the proper name Śāntinātha and the abstract 'peace' simultaneously — object of kittaṇaṁ.
saṁti-kittaṇaṁ = 'the proclaiming of Śānti/of peace', closing the couplet's parallel.

literalTranslation: O Ajita Jina — productive of well-being [is] the proclaiming of your name, O Supreme Person; and likewise productive of fortitude and wisdom — the proclaiming of your peace (Śānti), O Best of Jinas.
idiomaticTranslation: O unconquered Jina Ajita — to sing your name, O highest of beings, brings forth pure well-being; and so too, O finest of Jinas, to sing of you, O Śānti, brings forth steadfast resolve and clear wisdom, and the very peace your name proclaims.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the preceding stanza only sounded the bare homage ('namo ajiya saṁtiṇaṁ'), this verse names what that homage accomplishes — it tells the reciter the fruit of the praise itself. The stanza is built as a tight chiastic parallel: each half pairs a 'bringing-forth' (pavattaṇaṁ) with a 'proclaiming' (kittaṇaṁ). For Ajita, the kīrtana of his name (nāma-kittaṇaṁ) is suha-pavattaṇaṁ — generative of well-being; for Śānti, the kīrtana of his name (saṁti-kittaṇaṁ) is dhii-mai-ppavattaṇaṁ — generative of fortitude and discernment. The two epithets answer each other across the couplet: purisuttama (supreme person) for Ajita, jiṇuttama (best of Jinas) for Śānti. Note the deliberate triple-play on saṁti in 'saṁti kittaṇaṁ': it is at once the name Śāntinātha, the abstract peace he embodies, and the peace the chanting itself confers — so that to proclaim his name is already to receive what is proclaimed. The pairing dhii (dhṛti, steadfast resolve) with mai (mati, right understanding) is pointed: the two faculties a practitioner most needs to hold a vow and to see the path clearly. The verse thus moves the hymn from declaring homage to promising its concrete inner harvest, setting up vv.5–6's call to take refuge.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The stanza rests on the Jain understanding of nāma-kīrtana: the Jina, being vītarāga, bestows nothing materially; rather, dwelling on his name and qualities purifies the reciter's own bhāva, so the listed fruits — suha, dhṛti, mati, śānti — arise within the devotee, not as gifts handed down. Tellingly, every fruit named here is interior and soteriological (well-being, resolve, discernment, peace), never worldly gain. Dhṛti and mati form a classic Jain pairing of the conative and cognitive supports of right conduct: fortitude to sustain the vows, discernment to apply them — the very ground on which samyak-cāritra and samyak-jñāna stand.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: When reaching this stanza, the reciter can chant with explicit intent: let the saying of Ajita's name settle the mind into well-being, and the saying of Śānti's name strengthen perseverance (dhṛti) in one's vows and sharpen clear understanding (mati), so that the kīrtana itself becomes the peace it speaks of.

crossReferences.text: Logassa — 'kittaïssaṁ caüvīsaṁ pi kevalī' (I will proclaim the twenty-four), the same √kīrt act of naming the Jinas
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha ed.), Logassa sūtra

crossReferences.text: Ajitaśānti Stava v.3 refrain 'namo ajiya saṁtiṇaṁ', which this verse expands by naming the fruit of that homage

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit-English Dictionary (Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon)
sources.consultedFor: Root-forms and senses of pravartana (pavattaṇa), kīrtana (kittaṇa), dhṛti (dhii), mati (mai), and puruṣottama / jinottama.

sources.title: Ajitaśānti (Ajita-śānti) — definition and identification
sources.consultedFor: Identification of the Ajitaśānti Stava as a twin hymn to Ajitanātha and Śāntinātha and its place among Jain stotras.

sources.title: Ajitanatha — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Ajitanātha as the 2nd Tīrthaṅkara, supporting the reading of 'ajia' as both name and 'invincible'.

sources.title: Ajitshanti Stavanam (attributed to Nandiṣeṇa Sūri) — edition
sources.consultedFor: Attribution to Nandiṣeṇasūri and corroboration of the hymn's verse text in print editions.

notes: મઇ (mai) is read as mati ('intellect/discernment'), paired with ધિઇ (dhii = dhṛti); the homophonous Prakrit 'mai' = 'to me' is rejected here on contextual grounds. સંતિ (saṁti) is intentionally bivalent — Śāntinātha and 'peace' — and the rendering preserves both. The doubled initial conjunct in પ્પવત્તણં (ppavattaṇaṁ), arising by sandhi, is kept verbatim per the book's orthography and not normalized to match the single-conjunct પવત્તણં earlier in the verse. Recensions of this hymn vary slightly in spelling/anusvāra; the book's reading is followed. Web searches returned one fabricated 'full text' that did not match the authoritative verse and was disregarded.
